title: Focus on social structures: gender in medieval Muslim societies
description: Read passages and answer questions about the causes of gender hierarchies in medieval Muslim societies.
topic_path: Social institutions in the Islamic world
Focus on social structures: gender in medieval Muslim societies

assessment_items.question_content:
Before answering the question below, read the following passage:

> "There are myriad historical, geographical, socioeconomic, religious-doctrinal, and legal differences within the category of women and Islam. Much of the variation in the situations of women in the Muslim world is governed by class as well as religious, ethnic, and social considerations. Women of the same social class have similar life experiences across ethnic, national, or religious lines. Wealthy women and working-class women in different societies may have more in common with one another than with women of other classes in their own communities. In addition, women who are Muslims are affected by non-Muslim practices, beliefs, and cultures."

From Ira M. Lapidus, _A History of Islamic Societies_. New York: Cambridge University Press, 2014.

**Which of the following best summarizes the argument of this passage?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Lapidus thinks that socioeconomic and religious factors influence the lives of women.

choices.content: Wealthy women in the Muslim world had the same experiences
Gender conventions in the Muslim world were largely based on Islamic scripture
Women in the Muslim world had very different experiences, depending on things like class and social status.
Women’s lives in the Muslim world were not influenced by religion

assessment_items.question_content:
Before answering the question below, read the following passage:

> "There are myriad historical, geographical, socioeconomic, religious-doctrinal, and legal differences within the category of women and Islam. Much of the variation in the situations of women in the Muslim world is governed by class as well as religious, ethnic, and social considerations. Women of the same social class have similar life experiences across ethnic, national, or religious lines. Wealthy women and working-class women in different societies may have more in common with one another than with women of other classes in their own communities. In addition, women who are Muslims are affected by non-Muslim practices, beliefs, and cultures."

From Ira M. Lapidus, _A History of Islamic Societies_. New York: Cambridge University Press, 2014.

**What does Lapidus claim about the effect of class on the lives of women in the Muslim world?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: He notes that wealthy women in different societies had similar experiences

choices.content: Class was not as influential as ethnic, national, or religious factors.
Women of similar social classes often had similar experiences to one another.
Class was the sole determining factor of women’s experiences
Class factors only affected wealthy women

assessment_items.question_content:
Before answering the question below, read the following passage:

> "There are myriad historical, geographical, socioeconomic, religious-doctrinal, and legal differences within the category of women and Islam. Much of the variation in the situations of women in the Muslim world is governed by class as well as religious, ethnic, and social considerations. Women of the same social class have similar life experiences across ethnic, national, or religious lines. Wealthy women and working-class women in different societies may have more in common with one another than with women of other classes in their own communities. In addition, women who are Muslims are affected by non-Muslim practices, beliefs, and cultures."

From Ira M. Lapidus, _A History of Islamic Societies_. New York: Cambridge University Press, 2014.


**This passage is consistent with which of the following ideas?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Lapidus discusses cultural interaction between Muslim and non-Muslim societies

choices.content: Practices like veiling and seclusion were common in Muslim and non-Muslim societies, like the Byzantine Empire and Sassanid Persia
Women of all classes had the right to inherit property and initiate divorce
Arab and Persian women had different levels of engagement in the public sphere
Women in Muslim societies were not influenced by non-Muslim cultures
